Use `useEbookAnswers` in all Growth Sheet components.\n\n---\n\n### FILE 1: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/EbookLesson2.tsx`\n\nExact text from Lesson 2 (pages 6-10). Use `LessonShell` with `number=\"02\"`, `accentColor=\"disc-i\"`, `title='\"I See,\" said the Blind Man!'`, `intro=\"\"`.\n\nContent sections:\n\n**Section 1 - \"I See, said the Blind Man!\"**\nText: \"In the next few minutes, you will be able to \"see\" something that has been around you all of your life. You will learn something very simple and VERY POWERFUL about yourself and others! Read on, because you are about to see what I mean.\"\n\n\"When I was 36 years old, I had a blind spot in my life. That blind spot was \"people.\" Although I was quite successful, I still struggled to understand what made other people tick. This was true even though I was a people person. The truth is that I did not even have a clear understanding of myself! Thankfully, a friend of mine shared some powerful information with me and I want to share it with you. This information helped me to understand that each person sees life in his or her own way.\"\n\n\"You and I look at life from our OWN perspective. That is only natural. What if you could see life from the perspective of others? That would be \"super\" natural! You can, but first, you must recognize one basic fact. Keep reading!\"\n\n**Section 2 - DISCovery Insights box - \"People have their OWN built-in Perspective on life\"** (styled like the callout box in lesson 1)\n\nText inside: \"Each person's perspective is built in to who they are. Some people call it personality. Some refer to it as temperament. I am sure that some of your family and friends are VERY different from you. If you are like me, you have often asked yourself, \"Why did they do that?\" or \"What were they thinking?\" or \"What were they NOT thinking?\" A lack of understanding of both ourselves and others can lead to tension, disappointment, hurt feelings, unmet expectations and poor communication. There is a simple key to understanding personality styles that I would like to share with you. When you have the key, you will unlock the mystery of relationships and communication and enjoy a much better life.\"\n\n\"The key is to understand the Model of Human Behavior. The model that we teach was developed years ago and has been widely used and accepted. I believe that this is the best-known model ever developed to help people on an everyday basis. People can understand this simple model as well as remember it and apply it. I have seen this information change people's lives. That is why I have spent over 30 years of my life learning and teaching this material. I know what it has done for me, and I know what it can do for you. Everyone has blind spots. I love to see the sparkle in someone's eyes when that person finally 'gets it' and exclaims, \"Now I see it!\" You will too! Now let's dig in...\"\n\nAttribution: \"- Robert A. Rohm, Ph.D.\" (right-aligned, bold)\n\n**Section 3 - \"The Model of Human Behavior\"** heading\n\nText: \"The Model of Human Behavior is based on two foundational observations about how people normally behave. These are also known as two key perspectives or observations. These two P's can be easily remembered in the following manner:\"\n\n**Observation #1 card** (styled nicely):\nTitle: \"Observation # 1: The Pace Perspective\"\nText: \"Some people are more Outgoing, while others are more Reserved. You can think of this as each person's internal motor. Some people always seem ready to go and dive in. They engage their motor quickly. Some people would rather take their time before diving in. They engage their motor more slowly or more cautiously.\"\n\n**Observation #2 card**:\nTitle: \"Observation # 2: The Priority Perspective\"\nText: \"Some people are more Task-oriented, while others are more People-oriented. You can think of this as each person's compass that guides them. Some people are focused on getting something done; others are more tuned-in to the people around them and their feelings.\"\n\nNote paragraph: \"With both observations, we want to emphasize that these behavioral tendencies are neither right nor wrong or good or bad. They are just different. We are simply identifying normal behavior styles. People have different styles, and that is okay.\"\n\n\"We represent these two observations in the following diagrams.\"\n\n**Section 4 - Motor/Compass visual** (styled DISC grid):\nShow a 2x2 grid with labels: \"Outgoing\" top, \"Reserved\" bottom, \"Task-oriented\" left, \"People-oriented\" right. In the cells put DISC letters D (top-left), I (top-right), C (bottom-left), S (bottom-right) — each with their disc color.\n\nAdd text below the grid:\n\"Thus, we have four behavioral tendencies to help us characterize people:\n• Outgoing\n• Reserved  \n• Task-oriented\n• People-oriented\"\n\n\"Of course, everyone has some of all four of these tendencies at different times and in different situations. However, most people typically have one or two of these tendencies that seem to fit them well in their everyday behavior. On the other hand, one or two of these tendencies usually do not fit them well. These tendencies may even seem foreign to their approach to life. The balance of the four tendencies shapes the way each person \"sees\" life and those around them. This is their built-in perspective!\"\n\n\"By combining the two previous diagrams, we can show four basic quadrants of the circle as shown below:\"\n\n\"Thus, the four basic personality traits emerge as shown in the above diagram corresponding to the four quadrants of the circle:\n• Outgoing and Task-oriented (upper left)\n• Outgoing and People-oriented (upper right)\n• Reserved and People-oriented (lower right)\n• Reserved and Task-oriented (lower left)\"\n\n\"We will explain more about these personality styles in the next lesson. Hang in there! You only need to learn a little more information, and then you will have developed new insights which means you will have the ability to see things that others often overlook. This will lead you towards BETTER RELATIONSHIPS AND COMMUNICATION! You will be able to use this vital information for the rest of your life!\"\n\n---\n\n### FILE 2: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/EbookLesson3.tsx`\n\nUse `LessonShell` with `number=\"03\"`, `accentColor=\"disc-s\"`, `title=\"The Four Main Personality Styles\"`, `intro=\"\"`.\n\n**Section 1** - text:\n\"Wouldn't it be amazing if you could read someone else's mind? You would be able to really understand what that person is saying, what he or she is motivated by, and how to communicate more clearly with that person. While we cannot turn you into a mind reader, we can give you a practical way to identify the personality styles of others. When you know a person's main personality style, you will be able to put yourself into his or her own shoes. You will be able to better connect with who he or she really is and how he or she is internally wired. In other words, you will be able to better understand that person. So, let's get started ...\"\n\n\"You will recall that in Lesson # 2, we introduced the idea that people can be observed as having four main personality styles. Refer to the next diagram.\"\n\n**DISC model diagram** (same styled 2x2 grid):\nD: \"Dominant / Driving / Doer\"\nI: \"Inspiring / Interesting / Interactive\"  \nS: \"Supportive / Steady / Stable\"\nC: \"Cautious / Competent / Careful\"\n\n**Section 2** - text box (styled):\n\"Please notice on the previous page that there are words in bold letters around the circle which represent different behavior patterns: OUTGOING, RESERVED, TASK-ORIENTED, and PEOPLE-ORIENTED. Sometimes people are OUTGOING and sometimes they are RESERVED in their behavior. Sometimes people are TASK-ORIENTED, and sometimes they are PEOPLE-ORIENTED. The four personality types are like four parts of a pie. Also, you can see the letters D, I, S and C in each of the four sections of the pie chart. We can describe the basic behavior of the four personality types using words that begin with D, I, S and C. In fact, we refer to this Model of Human Behavior as the DISC Model of Human Behavior. Some of the words which describe each of the different personality types are shown in the diagram on the next page in the first column of each of the different types called \"Words to Describe\".\"\n\nBoxed summary:\n\"To summarize the Model of Human Behavior (in DISC order):\nD stands for the DOMINANT Type which is OUTGOING and TASK-ORIENTED.\nI stands for the INSPIRING Type which is OUTGOING and PEOPLE-ORIENTED.\nS stands for the SUPPORTIVE Type which is RESERVED and PEOPLE-ORIENTED.\nC stands for the CAUTIOUS Type which is RESERVED and TASK-ORIENTED.\"\n\nText: \"Think about it ... If you expect a naturally RESERVED person to act in an OUTGOING way, you may wonder why that person seems so hesitant. If you expect an extremely TASK-ORIENTED person to be very PEOPLE-ORIENTED, you may wonder why that person seems so rigid or \"mean\". Of course, people can adjust and grow in terms of their behavior, and everyone is a mixture of all four personality styles. The point to remember is that by recognizing other people's normal behavior patterns, allows you to adjust your own expectations when relating to them. You will also find that recognizing other people's normal behavior patterns allows you to adapt your own behavior to better meet their different NEEDS.\"\n\nText: \"You can learn how to adapt and adjust the way you relate to the people you know in order to better meet the NEEDS of others.\"\n\n\"Many books have been written on the topic of relationships. There is one common theme that seems to emerge from all of them, namely, you cannot control someone else, but you can control yourself! Here is some great news: understanding and applying the Model of Human Behavior, you can adapt the way you relate and genuinely have a better connection with other people. All you need to do to have a BETTER RELATIONSHIP and COMMUNICATION with a person is relate to that person in a way that makes them comfortable.\"\n\n\"Now let's break down each of the four personality styles one by one, so that you can develop an even better understanding.\"\n\n**Four personality type cards** - one per style, each with a table:\n\n**The Dominant (D) Type** (color: disc-d):\n\"The DOMINANT Type which is OUTGOING and TASK-ORIENTED\"\n| Words to Describe | Dominant, Direct, Demanding, Decisive, Determined, Doer |\n| Their Mind-set | Get it done! Make it happen! Play to win! Results! |\n| Their Likes | Activities, Competition, Hard work, Doing things, Challenges, Getting results, Being in charge, Accomplishing tasks |\n| They Are | Goal-oriented, Hard to please, Self-confident, Firm, Industrious, Performance conscious |\n| Motivated by | Challenge, Choices, Control |\n| Environment Needs | Freedom, Authority, Varied activities, Opportunities for advancement |\n| Communication Style | Straightforward communication |\n| Blind spots | Being sensitive to people, Being able to relax, Patience |\n\n**The Inspiring (I) Type** (color: disc-i):\n\"The INSPIRING Type which is OUTGOING and PEOPLE-ORIENTED\"\n| Words to Describe | Inspiring, Influencing, Important, Interactive, Impressive, Interested in people |\n| Their Mind-set | To be the star of the show ... fun and excitement! |\n| Their Likes | Exposure to people, Short-term projects, Making people laugh, Doing things, Lots of activities, To be on the go, Talking with people, Prestige, To be important |\n| They Are | Talkative, Great starters, Likeable, Prone to exaggerate, Easily excitable, Fun to watch |\n| Motivated by | Recognition, Approval, Popularity |\n| Environment Needs | Prestige, Friendly relationships, Opportunities to influence others, Opportunities to inspire others, Chances to verbalize ideas |\n| Communication Style | Friendly and informal communication |\n| Blind spots | Time management, Being unrealistic, Listening to others, Completion of tasks |\n\n**The Supportive (S) Type** (color: disc-s):\n\"The SUPPORTIVE Type which is RESERVED and PEOPLE-ORIENTED\"\n| Words to Describe | Supportive, Steady, Stable, Sweet, Sensitive, Sentimental |\n| Their Mind-set | Neutral. Let's all get along with each other! No conflict. |\n| Their Likes | Peace, An even pace, Harmony, Reassurance, Friendly groups, Teamwork, Helping others, Cooperation |\n| They Are | Team-oriented, Friendly, Cooperative, Loyal friends, Sensitive to others' needs, Understanding, Accepting |\n| Motivated by | Security, Appreciation, Assurance |\n| Environment Needs | An area of specialization, Identification with a group, Established work pattern, Stability of situation, Consistent environment |\n| Communication Style | Warm, open, sincere communication |\n| Blind spots | Dealing with change, Being able to say, \"No!\", Acting independently |\n\n**The Cautious (C) Type** (color: disc-c):\n\"The CAUTIOUS Type which is RESERVED and TASK-ORIENTED\"\n| Words to Describe | Cautious, Calculating, Competent, Consistent, Contemplative, Careful |\n| Their Mind-set | Let's do things right and with excellence. What is the plan? Have you thought things through? What is the purpose behind this? Why? |\n| Their Likes | Consistency, Excellent work, Being accurate, Information/Data, Value, Quality, Getting things right, Having a plan, Procedure, Honesty |\n| They Are | Procedure-oriented, Dedicated to the task, Focused on the details, Logical, Accurate, Respectful |\n| Motivated by | Quality answers, Excellence, Value |\n| Environment Needs | Clearly defined tasks, Sufficient time and resources to accomplish tasks, Freedom to ask questions, Limited risks, Assignments that require planning and precision |\n| Communication Style | Logical, precise and detailed communication |\n| Blind spots | Over-analyzing, Ability to meet deadlines, Perfectionism, The need to express feelings, The importance of the feelings of others |\n\nClosing text:\n\"That is the summary of the four personality styles. Now let's apply this information in Growth Sheet # 3.\"\n\n\"Here is what I want you to do next... answer the following questions on the next two pages. Again, refer back to the list of names that you used in the previous Growth Sheets for Lessons # 1 and # 2. This should take about five to ten minutes.\"\n\n\"Do not be surprised if you identify with some of the different traits found in all four of the different personality styles. That is because everyone is a unique blend of all four of these personality styles - to a greater or lesser degree. This information will transform the way you see people, understand people and hopefully interact with people.\"\n\n---\n\n### FILE 3: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/EbookLesson4.tsx`\n\nUse `LessonShell` with `number=\"04\"`, `accentColor=\"disc-c\"`, `title=\"Relating Better by Adapting to Others\"`, `intro=\"\"`.\n\n**Section 1**:\n\"This lesson is where the rubber meets the road! We have talked about the four personality styles and the DISC Model of Human Behavior. Now, we are going to talk about how you can use the DISC information in everyday life. My passion has been to make DISC easy to understand and simple to apply. With the charts included in this lesson, you will have a simple guide to adapt your behavior to enjoy BETTER RELATIONSHIPS and COMMUNICATION. Please read this last lesson with an open mind and a willingness to adjust your own behavior. When you learn how to do this, you will certainly be able to improve the way you connect with other people.\"\n\n**DISCovery Insights box**:\n\"First, let me be totally honest. There were times in the past when I used this information as a way to label other people and made excuses for myself. Labels and excuses never led me to BETTER RELATIONSHIPS and COMMUNICATION. This information has been tremendously helpful when I have used it as a **tool to build bridges** of communication rather than as an **excuse to build walls** that hinder communication. I share this with you because I am convinced that a sincere and genuine desire to connect with someone is the purpose for using this information. We are not going to try to fix anyone else. We are going to learn to adjust our own behavior. With this in mind, let's get going.\"\n\n**Section 2**:\n\"You will recall in Lesson # 2, we introduced the idea that people can be observed as having four main personality styles. Some styles are more predominant and other styles are less predominant. Refer to the following diagram.\"\n\nShow the DISC 2x2 diagram again (same as before).\n\n\"In order to adapt to another person to build better bridges of communication, it is important to understand the dynamics of how different personality styles interact. There are some simple charts in this lesson that will show you what these dynamics look like. The charts identify some of the natural **strengths** from which your relationships can benefit. They also identify some of the **struggles** that you are likely to face in the relationship. Lastly, they provide some **strategies** on interacting with others in a way that will lead to BETTER RELATIONSHIPS and COMMUNICATION. The four charts are shown on the following pages. Pick the one that corresponds to your primary personality type. For example, if you are primarily a Dominant (D) type, you will pick the first set of charts on pages 22 - 23. Read over the chart that applies to you, and then proceed to Growth Sheet # 4 on pages 30 - 31.\"\n\nThen show an interactive style selector (tabs for D, I, S, C). When a type is selected, show the 3 charts for that type (how they relate to D, I, S, C). Use the EXACT content below.\n\n**D type charts** (show when D is selected):\n\nD→D:\nStrengths: Your mutual goals, admiration and the desire to get results can be very positive and affirming.\nStruggles: Power struggles are the most challenging. Neither of you wants to back down, give up or compromise.\nStrategies: Don't force issues. Allow this person to have some choices, control and authority. Don't argue or give ultimatums. Be direct and stick to business.\n\nD→I:\nStrengths: Both of you are fast paced. This person may desire to please you and follow your leadership.\nStruggles: Your focus on getting things done can clash with this person's desire to have fun and \"take-life-as-it-comes.\" This person does not share your drive to complete tasks. This person's focus is on people rather than on tasks.\nStrategies: Realize that this person does not usually focus on one thing, rather he or she focuses on many things. Help Inspiring (I) types finish tasks by working WITH them. Make things FUN! Allow them to talk and socialize. Be positive and willing to express approval to them. Be accepting of their expressions of emotion and feelings.\n\nD→S:\nStrengths: You like to lead, and this personality type likes to follow and help. A supportive person will feel secure as long as you show controlled, stable behavior.\nStruggles: If you come on too strong, this person can feel intimidated and will take it personally. You may misunderstand this person's softhearted, easygoing nature as being \"weak.\" Never confuse kindness for weakness. Remember that this person's focus is on people, and he or she tends to be slower paced.\nStrategies: Be patient and willing to spell things out, step-by-step when working on tasks. Communicate in a calmer, softer manner. Relax, and do not push. Express appreciation often. Be sincere.\n\nD→C:\nStrengths: Both of you focus on tasks and enjoy working independently. With this person's attention to detail, you can accomplish a lot together.\nStruggles: You tend to move quickly, whereas this person likes to think things through. Your focus is to get things done now, and his or her focus is to get things done right. Your desire to control things may discourage a cautious person, because this person does not like to feel pressured.\nStrategies: Do not rush or push this person. Do not criticize a cautious person. Be patient, and give him or her time to make decisions. Be willing to answer this person's questions and provide information in a polite manner. Do not expect this person to be a risk-taker like you.\n\n**I type charts** (show when I is selected):\n\nI→D:\nStrengths: You both are outgoing and activity-driven. Both of you like to win. You may admire the strengths and achievements of this person.\nStruggles: You may find the other person to be too controlling, while you are too permissive. You are more social, while the other person is more task-driven.\nStrategies: Understand that a D-type person is direct and results-oriented. Be more direct and get to the point with him or her. Do not be afraid of confrontation. Expect it, and do not take it personally. Work first, and THEN have fun.\n\nI→I:\nStrengths: Both of you live enthusiastically and enjoy being with people. You both like to have fun and tend to forgive easily.\nStruggles: Both of you tend to live emotionally and may compete for attention. You both are impulsive, and that can lead to challenges with following through with responsibilities and staying organized.\nStrategies: Remember to listen to the other person, because he or she likes to talk as much as you do. When working on important tasks, keep each other accountable, and be clear about who is responsible for what. Give sincere recognition of this person's abilities, ideas and contributions.\n\nI→S:\nStrengths: Both of you are people-oriented. You like to talk and the other person likes to listen. You tend to get along very well.\nStruggles: Most struggles are related to pace. You like things fast-paced, exciting, spontaneous and with high energy. The other person likes things to be more calm, quiet and predictable.\nStrategies: Slow down your approach. Tone down your enthusiasm. Be sincere with praise and appreciation. Do not embarrass this person in public. Allow this person time to warm up and open up to you.\n\nI→C:\nStrengths: Your opposite strengths provide a good balance to each other's weaknesses. You can learn from the other person's analytical nature, and this person can learn not to take things so seriously and to have more fun.\nStruggles: Your differences can lead to misunderstandings. You love to talk and be on the go, but the other person likes time alone. You are much more verbal, and it is easy for you to miss the other person's more indirect way of sharing concerns.\nStrategies: Tone down your emotional reactions. Be more factual and objective, especially in the face of conflict. Do not rush or push this person. Be specific in your communication, and expect this person to speak literally and to take things literally.\n\n**S type charts** (show when S is selected):\n\nS→D:\nStrengths: You are a good supporter and encourager for this driven person who seeks to achieve and exert leadership.\nStruggles: This person can exhaust you by being controlling or by expecting instant action. You like to relax and go slow, but the other person does everything with a sense of urgency. You can become stressed, and this dominant person can become impatient.\nStrategies: Do not take it personally when this person takes action without you. Be more firm and results-oriented with this person. Be more direct, decisive and action-oriented when you deal with this person.\n\nS→I:\nStrengths: You tend to get along well, because you are both people-oriented. You both provide praise and appreciation to each other which you each need to feel good about yourselves.\nStruggles: Your biggest struggle will be keeping up with the pace of this person. The I-type likes excitement and activity, but you like things to be slower and calmer. Inspiring (I) type people have a large social circle, and that can seem overwhelming to you.\nStrategies: Be more outgoing and energetic with this person. An inspiring person is very impulsive. Be careful not to let this person talk you into doing something you do not want to do. Set some limits and do not feel pressured by this person's energy. Realize that he or she moves quickly, so do not take it personally if this person seems to give you fragmented attention.\n\nS→S:\nStrengths: You have a lot in common and enjoy being with each other. Both of you like a relaxed, personal atmosphere.\nStruggles: The main struggle is in the area of communication. You both talk indirectly and do not insist on your own way. Neither of you likes to make hard decisions. Neither of you likes conflict or tension, so you avoid bringing up unpleasant issues.\nStrategies: Be willing to take more initiative and to be more decisive. Realize that some conflict and change is healthy. Draw out how the other person feels, and be willing to honestly share how you feel. Do not hide hurt feelings. Get issues and feelings out on the table and discuss them.\n\nS→C:\nStrengths: Both of you are slower paced. Neither of you is \"pushy\" and you both prefer to avoid conflict. You can enjoy being together without a lot of conversation.\nStruggles: You tend to be sensitive while the other person has a tendency toward being critical. Your feelings-oriented nature can clash with the other person's logic-oriented nature. You want warm relationships, but the other person can seem more cold and impersonal to you.\nStrategies: Do not take this person's questioning, critical nature personally. This person likes to think deeply and analyze everything. Be willing to give in-depth answers. Do not push this person into closeness. Realize that this person is more Task-oriented than People-oriented, so he or she may not be as warm or sensitive as you are.\n\n**C type charts** (show when C is selected):\n\nC→D:\nStrengths: Both of you share a similar view toward accomplishing tasks. As long as you share the same goals, you can be very effective as a team.\nStruggles: You may have conflict if you take different approaches to accomplishing a task. You want things done \"right,\" and this person is focused on getting things done quickly. They may think that you are over-analyzing things, while you may think that he or she is being too hasty.\nStrategies: Accept the fact that the D-type person needs to have some control and the ability to take some action. Do not criticize or expect perfection. Instead, be willing to recognize and affirm this person's accomplishments.\n\nC→I:\nStrengths: Your strengths balance each other out. You need the other person's freshness and fun, and the other person needs your discipline and logic.\nStruggles: Because you are opposites in personality, you may have a hard time understanding each other. You may not relate to this person's talkative, outgoing nature, and he or she may not relate to your analytical, cautious nature. Your standards may be too high for this person. You may naturally withhold the praise on which this person thrives.\nStrategies: You must modify your expectations of this person. Realize that this person will never have the attention to detail that you do. Look for this person's strengths, and be generous with recognition and approval. Listen enthusiastically to his or her stories. Most of all, do not push for perfection or this person may become discouraged and quit.\n\nC→S:\nStrengths: Both of you like to take things slowly. You both enjoy a low-key relationship that is free from conflict.\nStruggles: You may become frustrated when this person does not appear to think things through the way you do or share your enthusiasm for key details. This person is feelings-oriented, so you may come across as cold and impersonal.\nStrategies: Be aware of your focus on doing tasks correctly. This person's focus is on peace and security in relationships. Be more warm and personal. Be careful not to criticize. Show sincere appreciation for any effort that is made. Do not set your standards too high, or this person may feel inadequate or simply give up.\n\nC→C:\nStrengths: Both of you like to work hard on projects and focus on details and quality. You both tend to be serious and factual in your conversations.\nStruggles: There can be trouble when of you disagree on what is \"right.\" Both of you can quickly shut down and withdraw. You both tend to wage a war of indirect communication.\nStrategies: Be open and flexible when this person suggests a different way of doing something. Be very careful with any criticism, because you know that criticism of your own work is one of your greatest fears. Do not set your standards so high that the other person feels he or she may not be able to reach them. Be specific with words of encouragement. Tell this person exactly what he or she did correctly and why you liked it.\n\n---\n\n### FILE 4: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/GrowthSheet1.tsx`\n\nImport `useEbookAnswers` from `@/hooks/useEbookAnswers`.\n\nTitle: \"Better Relationships Growth Sheet # 1\" (use a styled header like a lesson header, but with disc-d accent)\n\n**Question 1**: \nText: \"1. List four people in your life that are important to you. (There may be more, but for now, let's stick with four.) For the first three people choose individuals whom you like and with whom you seem to get along fairly well. For the fourth person choose an individual with whom you seem to often have the most difficult challenge.\"\n\nShow 4 text inputs (for `gs1_person1Name`, `gs1_person2Name`, `gs1_person3Name`, `gs1_person4Name`) with labels \"Person # 1:\", \"Person # 2:\", \"Person # 3:\", \"Person # 4:\".\n\n**Question 2**:\nText: \"2. For the first three people, in your own words, write down a very brief description of each of their personality traits and what you think their greatest strength might be. (This is NOT a test. Just write down what you think. You will refer back to this list in the upcoming lessons.)\"\n\nShow 3 textareas for `gs1_person1Traits`, `gs1_person2Traits`, `gs1_person3Traits` with labels \"Person # 1:\", \"Person # 2:\", \"Person # 3:\".\n\n**Question 3**:\nText: \"3. Finally, choose Person # 4 from the previous page and in your own words explain why you think you may have a very difficult relationship. (Suggestion: pick someone you deal with often.)\"\n\nShow 1 textarea for `gs1_person4Why` with label \"Person # 4:\".\n\n**Closing text**:\n\"In Lesson # 2, you will learn a new way to look at how to understand personalities and how other people act, interact and react in different situations.\"\n\"Congratulations, you are on a new journey towards BETTER RELATIONSHIPS and COMMUNICATION! We have designed the lessons to be both practical yet applicable. You will be glad that you completed the lessons as you begin to apply this information. It really does work!\"\n\n---\n\n### FILE 5: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/GrowthSheet2.tsx`\n\nTitle: \"Better Personal Relationships and Communication Growth Sheet # 2\"\n\nImport `useEbookAnswers`.\n\n**Instruction text**:\n\"Here is what I want you to do next... answer the multiple-choice questions below. This will only take a few minutes. Earlier, I said that the key is understanding the Model of Human Behavior. It is more accurate to say that the key is applying what you learn! This next short exercise is an important part of applying this information.\"\n\n\"Note: As I stated previously, everyone has some of all four of these tendencies at different times and in different situations. For now, simply get started by choosing the traits that you believe fit you most of the time. It is okay to simply take an \"educated guess\" even if you are not 100% sure. This is called practice! Medical Doctors \"practice\" medicine daily. It is okay for you to practice reading people daily until you get better at doing it.\"\n\n**Step 1** - \"Start with yourself! Write your name in the blank below. Next, mark the traits that seem to fit you most of the time.\"\n\nShow:\n- Text input for `gs2_yourName` labeled \"Your name:\"\n- Two toggle button groups:\n  - Pace: \"Outgoing\" | \"Reserved\" (stored in `gs2_yourPace`)\n  - Priority: \"Task-oriented\" | \"People-oriented\" (stored in `gs2_yourPriority`)\n- Small note: \"Remember, you are just practicing, and it is okay to guess - even if you are not 100% sure.\"\n\n**Step 2** - \"2. Refer back to the three people that you listed in Growth Sheet # 1 in the previous lesson. Next, mark the traits that seem to fit them most of the time (as in Step 1 above). Remember, you are just practicing, and it is okay to guess - even if you are not 100% sure.\"\n\nShow persons 1, 2, 3 — each with:\n- Name display (read-only, from `answers.gs1_personXName`)\n- Text input for name (editable, stored `gs2_p1Name` etc. — but actually just show the name from GS1)\n- Pace toggle: Outgoing | Reserved\n- Priority toggle: Task-oriented | People-oriented\n\n**Step 3** - \"3. Finally, refer back to the name of the person that you wrote down in Lesson # 1 (on page five) with whom you have a difficult relationship. Next, mark the traits that seem to fit him or her most of the time.\"\n\nShow Person #4 with same toggles.\n\nClosing: \"In Lesson # 3, we will look at the four main personality styles in depth. Please stick with these next two short lessons! This will help you to better understand the Model of Human Behavior. Lesson # 4 will show you how to apply your knowledge with the important people you listed. Just in case you need to be reminded - It is okay to simply take an \"educated guess\" even if you are not 100% sure. This is called practice! Medical Doctors \"practice\" medicine daily. It is okay for you to practice reading people daily until you get better at doing it.\"\n\n\"You are one step closer to enjoying BETTER RELATIONSHIPS!\"\n\n---\n\n### FILE 6: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/GrowthSheet3.tsx`\n\nTitle: \"Better Personal Relationships and Communication Growth Sheet # 3\"\n\nImport `useEbookAnswers`.\n\n**Step 1 instruction**: \"1. Write your name in the blank below. Next, refer to the previous charts in Growth Sheet # 2 and fill in the information for your motivation, environment needs, communication style and blind spot. Use the \"DISCovery Insights Information\" previously found on pages 15 - 17 to help you better understand different aspects of your own personality style. You will also use this information to help you identify traits regarding other personality styles.\"\n\nShow for \"Yourself\":\n- Name display from `answers.gs2_yourName` (read-only label showing the name)\n- DISC style selector (4 clickable buttons D/I/S/C) → stored in `gs3_yourStyle`\n- Note: \"Remember, you are just practicing, and it is okay to guess your DISC personality style - even if you are not 100% sure.\"\n- Text input: \"I am motivated by:\" → `gs3_yourMotivatedBy`\n- Text input: \"My environment needs are:\" → `gs3_yourEnvironmentNeeds`\n- Text input: \"My communication style is:\" → `gs3_yourCommStyle`\n- Text input: \"One blind spot in myself that I can work on is:\" → `gs3_yourBlindSpot`\n\n**Step 2** - \"2. Refer back to the different people that you listed in Growth Sheet # 1. Write the names of those people below. Continue the exercise just like you did in Question # 1 above.\"\n\nShow same fields for Person #1, Person #2, Person #3 (using `gs3_p1Style`, `gs3_p1MotivatedBy`, etc.)\n\n**Step 3** (from page 19) - \"3. Finally, refer back to the name of the person you wrote down in Growth Sheet # 1 with whom you feel like you have a difficult relationship. Write this person's name below. Continue the exercise just like you did in Question # 1 on the previous page.\"\n\nShow same fields for Person #4.\n\nClosing: \"In the last lesson (Lesson # 4), we will look at how YOU CAN ADJUST your own style to the four main personality styles. Only one more lesson to go! Lesson # 4 will show you about your **strengths**, **struggles** and **strategies** for working with other people. I am excited about your progress in developing BETTER RELATIONSHIPS and COMMUNICATION!\"\n\n---\n\n### FILE 7: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/GrowthSheet4.tsx`\n\nTitle: \"Better Personal Relationships and Communication Growth Sheet # 4\"\n\nImport `useEbookAnswers`.\n\n**Intro text**: \"Now let's apply this information in our following Growth Sheet.\"\n\n\"**Here is what I want you to do next**… answer the questions below. Again, refer back to the list of names that you used in the previous Growth Sheets. This should take about 5 minutes.\"\n\n**Step 1** - \"1. Refer back to the four people that you listed in the Growth Sheets (pages 18 -19, not counting yourself). Write their names and their style below. Then from the previous pages in Lesson # 4 (pages 20 -29) look at **your own** personality's Guide to Better Relationships and Communication chart and list your strengths, struggles and strategies in your relationship **with the personality of the four people** that you listed. Refer to each specific chart, but feel free to use your own words.\"\n\nShow for Persons 1, 2, 3:\n- Name display (from GS1 answers, read-only)\n- DISC style selector (clickable D/I/S/C) → `gs4_p1Style`, etc.\n- \"Remember, you are just practicing...\" note\n- Text input: \"Strength of your relationship:\" → `gs4_p1Strength`\n- Text input: \"Struggle of your relationship:\" → `gs4_p1Struggle`\n- Text input: \"Strategy of your relationship:\" → `gs4_p1Strategy`\n\n**Step 2** - \"2. Finally, refer back to the name of the person you wrote down in the previous Growth Sheets (page 19) with whom you feel like you have a **difficult relationship**. Write that name below. Continue the exercise just like you did with the other 3 people.\"\n\nShow for Person 4 same fields.\n\nThen display the completion section:\n\"**Congratulations!!** You have completed the four part course on **Better Relationships and Communication.** I hope that you are excited about applying what you have learned so far!\"\n\n**Highlights to Remember** box (styled nicely):\n\"1) You cannot BEWARE of something until you are first AWARE of something.\n2) People have their own built-in perspective on life.\n3) We can adapt our behavior to better meet the needs of others.\n4) DISC is a tool to build bridges for communication.\n5) A sincere and genuine desire to connect with someone is the key to using this information correctly.\"\n\nClosing paragraphs:\n\"You cannot control anyone else, but you can control yourself. Above all else, remember Dr. Rohm's only Rule for Personality Styles: \"The one who knows this information is the one responsible for using it!\" In other words, you cannot expect someone who does not know this information to use it. Since you are the one who now knows it, the ball is in your court to use it. You will be the change agent as well as the winner for using this information!\"\n\n\"I am excited that you can now use the DISC information to help you enjoy BETTER RELATIONSHIPS and COMMUNICATION!!\"\n\n\"All of life is about two things...learning and growing. You are on a great journey. Keep learning and keep growing!\"\n\n\"- Robert A. Rohm, Ph.D.\" (right-aligned, bold)\n\n---\n\n### ALSO UPDATE: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/EbookReader.tsx`\n\nExpand the page list to include all new pages. Import all new components. The new page order should be:\n\n```typescript\ntype EbookPage = \"cover\" | \"toc\" | \"lesson1\" | \"growth1\" | \"lesson2\" | \"lesson3\" | \"lesson4\" | \"growth2\" | \"growth3\" | \"growth4\";\n```\n\nWait - actually better order:\n```\n\"cover\" | \"toc\" | \"lesson1\" | \"growth1\" | \"lesson2\" | \"growth2\" | \"lesson3\" | \"growth3\" | \"lesson4\" | \"growth4\"\n```\n\nPAGE_ORDER: [\"cover\", \"toc\", \"lesson1\", \"growth1\", \"lesson2\", \"growth2\", \"lesson3\", \"growth3\", \"lesson4\", \"growth4\"]\n\nPAGE_LABELS: cover→\"Cover\", toc→\"Contents\", lesson1→\"Lesson 1\", growth1→\"Growth Sheet 1\", lesson2→\"Lesson 2\", growth2→\"Growth Sheet 2\", lesson3→\"Lesson 3\", growth3→\"Growth Sheet 3\", lesson4→\"Lesson 4\", growth4→\"Growth Sheet 4\"\n\nPAGE_COLORS: lesson1→\"bg-disc-d\", growth1→\"bg-disc-d\", lesson2→\"bg-disc-i\", growth2→\"bg-disc-i\", lesson3→\"bg-disc-s\", growth3→\"bg-disc-s\", lesson4→\"bg-disc-c\", growth4→\"bg-disc-c\"\n\nUpdate content rendering to show GrowthSheet1, GrowthSheet2, etc.\n\n---\n\n### ALSO UPDATE: `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/EbookTOC.tsx`\n\nRead the file first and update to include the growth sheets in navigation.\n\n---\n\n### STYLING GUIDELINES for all components:\n\nFor inputs/textareas in Growth Sheets:\n```tsx\n\u003ctextarea\n  value={answers.KEY ?? \"\"}\n  onChange={(e) =\u003e setAnswer(\"KEY\", e.target.value)}\n  className=\"w-full min-h-[80px] rounded-lg border border-border/50 bg-muted/20 px-3 py-2.5 text-sm text-foreground placeholder:text-muted-foreground/40 resize-none focus:outline-none focus:ring-1 focus:ring-disc-d/50\"\n  placeholder=\"Type here...\"\n/\u003e\n```\n\nFor text inputs:\n```tsx\n\u003cinput\n  type=\"text\"\n  value={answers.KEY ?? \"\"}\n  onChange={(e) =\u003e setAnswer(\"KEY\", e.target.value)}\n  className=\"w-full rounded-lg border border-border/50 bg-muted/20 px-3 py-2.5 text-sm text-foreground placeholder:text-muted-foreground/40 focus:outline-none focus:ring-1 focus:ring-disc-d/50\"\n  placeholder=\"Type here...\"\n/\u003e\n```\n\nFor DISC style selector buttons:\n```tsx\n{([\"D\", \"I\", \"S\", \"C\"] as const).map((letter) =\u003e {\n  const colors = { D: \"disc-d\", I: \"disc-i\", S: \"disc-s\", C: \"disc-c\" };\n  const color = colors[letter];\n  const selected = answers.KEY === letter;\n  return (\n    \u003cbutton\n      key={letter}\n      onClick={() =\u003e setAnswer(\"KEY\", letter)}\n      className={`h-9 w-9 rounded-lg font-bold text-sm transition-all border ${\n        selected\n          ? `bg-${color} text-white border-${color}`\n          : \"bg-muted/30 text-muted-foreground border-border/40 hover:bg-muted/50\"\n      }`}\n    \u003e\n      {letter}\n    \u003c/button\u003e\n  );\n})}\n```\n\nFor Outgoing/Reserved and Task/People toggles:\n```tsx\n{[\"Outgoing\", \"Reserved\"].map((opt) =\u003e (\n  \u003cbutton\n    key={opt}\n    onClick={() =\u003e setAnswer(\"KEY\", opt.toLowerCase())}\n    className={`flex-1 py-2 rounded-lg text-xs font-semibold transition-all border ${\n      answers.KEY === opt.toLowerCase()\n        ? \"bg-disc-i text-white border-disc-i\"\n        : \"bg-muted/30 text-muted-foreground border-border/40\"\n    }`}\n  \u003e\n    {opt}\n  \u003c/button\u003e\n))}\n```\n\nFor section labels use: `\u003cp className=\"text-[11px] font-semibold text-muted-foreground/60 uppercase tracking-wide mb-1\"\u003e`\n\nFor the growth sheet heading use a div like:\n```tsx\n\u003cdiv className=\"px-6 pb-12 pt-8 space-y-8 max-w-lg mx-auto\"\u003e\n  \u003cdiv className=\"rounded-xl bg-muted/40 border border-border/30 px-4 py-2.5 mb-6\"\u003e\n    \u003cp className=\"text-xs font-bold text-foreground\"\u003eBetter Personal Relationships and Communication\u003c/p\u003e\n    \u003cp className=\"text-sm font-bold text-foreground\"\u003eGrowth Sheet # X\u003c/p\u003e\n  \u003c/div\u003e\n  ...\n\u003c/div\u003e\n```\n\nIMPORTANT notes:\n- Do NOT use `subtitle` prop on LessonShell (it doesn't exist) — use `intro=\"\"` for empty intros\n- All growth sheet components should use `useEbookAnswers` hook\n- For D type table/info sections in EbookLesson3, use a styled table or card layout (not actual HTML table)\n- Keep consistent spacing and dark theme styling\n- Run typecheck after writing all files to fix any errors","subagent_type":"vibecode-webapp-plugin:frontend-developer"}

### Vibecode Agent
*4/2/2026, 9:56:50 AM*

Everything is done! Here's what was updated:

**Fixed:**
- Cover now shows: "Developed by Robert A. Rohm, Ph.D. and Patrick D. Pettibon / Editors: JJ Brun and Gina Floyd"

**Exact PDF text** is now used throughout all 4 lessons — no more AI-invented content. The text is organized across 10 pages for easier reading:
- Lesson 1 → Growth Sheet 1 → Lesson 2 → Growth Sheet 2 → Lesson 3 → Growth Sheet 3 → Lesson 4 → Growth Sheet 4

**Interactive Growth Sheets:**
- Tap **D / I / S / C** buttons to select a personality style (instead of circling on paper)
- Tap **Outgoing / Reserved** and **Task-oriented / People-oriented** toggle buttons
- All text fields are fully typeable
- Answers auto-save to your account within 1.5 seconds of typing — they'll be there every time you open the ebook

**Page 32** (the advertisement page) is not included.

{"description":"Fix TOC centering and progressive lesson lock","id":"019d4ebc-353b-7744-bcc8-13b31d74a395","prompt":"I need you to make two changes to the ebook reader components.\n\n## Task 1: TOC Desktop Centering\n\nIn `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/EbookTOC.tsx`, the content is too far left on desktop. Add a `max-w-sm mx-auto` wrapper around the content (or change the outer div to constrain width) so it looks centered on wider screens. The current outer div has `px-6`. Change it so the content is centered with wider margins on desktop.\n\nRead the file first, then make the change.\n\n## Task 2: Progressive Lesson Lock in EbookReader\n\nIn `/home/user/workspace/webapp/src/components/ebook/EbookReader.tsx`, implement sequential lesson locking:\n\n1. Track `maxUnlockedIndex` in state (the highest page index the user has reached). Initialize from `localStorage` key `\"ebook_max_page\"`. Default to `1` (so \"toc\" at index 1 is accessible, meaning users start at lesson1 from TOC but can reach toc freely - actually default to `0` so cover is the start, but toc should be reachable).\n\nActually, here's the exact logic:\n- `PAGE_ORDER = [\"cover\", \"toc\", \"lesson1\", \"growth1\", \"lesson2\", \"growth2\", \"lesson3\", \"growth3\", \"lesson4\", \"growth4\"]`\n- Index 0 = cover, Index 1 = toc, Index 2 = lesson1...\n- Default `maxUnlockedIndex` should start at `1` (cover + toc always accessible, must start at lesson1 which is index 2)\n- Actually make it so the user can freely navigate to \"cover\" and \"toc\" always, but for lessons/growth sheets they must proceed sequentially.\n- When user clicks Next button (`goForward`), update `maxUnlockedIndex` to `Math.max(maxUnlockedIndex, currentIndex + 1)` and save to localStorage.\n- Once the user reaches the last page (`growth4`, index 9), set a `ebookCompleted` flag in localStorage and in state. When `ebookCompleted` is true, all pages are accessible from TOC.\n- Pass to `EbookTOC` the info about which pages are locked.\n\nIn `EbookTOC.tsx`, add a `maxUnlockedIndex` prop and `ebookCompleted` prop. Lock (disable/show lock icon) TOC items whose index (in PAGE_ORDER) is greater than `maxUnlockedIndex`. When locked, show a lock icon and make the button `opacity-40 cursor-not-allowed` and don't call `onNavigate`.\n\nImportant: Cover (index 0) and TOC (index 1) should always be accessible (or just always show without lock).\n\nHere's the implementation plan:\n\n### EbookReader.tsx changes:\n1. Add `useState` for `maxUnlockedIndex` initialized from `parseInt(localStorage.getItem(\"ebook_max_page\") ?? \"1\", 10)` (default 1)\n2. Add `useState` for `ebookCompleted` initialized from `localStorage.getItem(\"ebook_completed\") === \"true\"`\n3. In `goForward`, after updating page: `const newMax = Math.max(maxUnlockedIndex, currentIndex + 1); setMaxUnlockedIndex(newMax); localStorage.setItem(\"ebook_max_page\", String(newMax)); if (PAGE_ORDER[currentIndex + 1] === \"growth4\" was already reached or newMax \u003e= 9) { ... }` — actually set ebookCompleted when user navigates TO growth4 (index 9) or beyond. So when `newMax \u003e= 9`, set completed.\n4. Pass `maxUnlockedIndex` and `ebookCompleted` to both `EbookTOC` usages.\n\n### EbookTOC.tsx changes:\n1. Add `maxUnlockedIndex?: number` and `ebookCompleted?: boolean` props to interface\n2. Import `Lock` from lucide-react\n3. For each chapter item, check if it's locked: `const pageIndex = PAGE_ORDER.indexOf(ch.page); const isLocked = !ebookCompleted \u0026\u0026 pageIndex \u003e maxUnlockedIndex;`\n4. Need to import PAGE_ORDER from EbookReader or re-define the order.\n5. If locked: render button with `opacity-40 cursor-not-allowed pointer-events-none` and show a `\u003cLock className=\"h-3.5 w-3.5\" /\u003e` instead of the Check icon.\n\nFor the PAGE_ORDER in TOC, since TOC only lists chapters (not cover/toc pages), you can compute it differently. The CHAPTERS array maps to pages: lesson1=2, growth1=3, lesson2=4, growth2=5, lesson3=6, growth3=7, lesson4=8, growth4=9. You can hardcode a page-to-index map or import PAGE_ORDER.\n\nThe cleanest approach: export `PAGE_ORDER` from `EbookReader.tsx` and import it in `EbookTOC.tsx`.\n\nPlease read both files first, then implement all changes.","subagent_type":"vibecode-webapp-plugin:frontend-developer"}

Many books have been written on the topic of relationships. There is one common theme that seems to emerge from all of them, namely, you cannot control someone else, but you can control yourself! Here is some great news: understanding and applying the Model of Human Behavior, you can adapt the way you relate and genuinely have a better connection with other people. Now let's break down each of the four personality styles one by one, so that you can develop an even better understanding."


All of this text is suppose to be above the "Discovery insights informaiton" text: "Please notice on the previous page that there are words in
bold letters around the circle which represent different behavior
patterns: OUTGOING, RESERVED, TASK-ORIENTED, and PEOPLEORIENTED.
Sometimes people are OUTGOING and sometimes
they are RESERVED in their behavior. Sometimes people are TASKORIENTED,
and sometimes they are PEOPLE-ORIENTED. The four
personality types are like four parts of a pie. Also, you can see the
letters D, I, S and C in each of the four sections of the pie chart. We
can describe the basic behavior of the four personality types using
words that begin with D, I, S and C. In fact, we refer to this Model
of Human Behavior as the DISC Model of Human Behavior. Some
of the words which describe each of the different personality types
are shown in the diagram on the next page in the first column of
each of the different types called “Words to Describe”.
D stands for the DOMINANT Type which is OUTGOING and TASK-ORIENTED.
I stands for the INSPIRING Type which is OUTGOING and PEOPLE-ORIENTED.
S stands for the SUPPORTIVE Type which is RESERVED and PEOPLE-ORIENTED.
C stands for the CAUTIOUS Type which is RESERVED and TASK-ORIENTED.
Think about it ... If you expect a naturally RESERVED person to
act in an OUTGOING way, you may wonder why that person seems
so hesitant. If you expect an extremely TASK-ORIENTED person
to be very PEOPLE-ORIENTED, you may wonder why that person
seems so rigid or “mean”. Of course, people can adjust and grow
in terms of their behavior, and everyone is a mixture of all four
personality styles. The point to remember is that by recognizing
other people’s normal behavior patterns, allows you to adjust your
own expectations when relating to them. You will also find that
recognizing other people’s normal behavior patterns allows you to
adapt your own behavior to better meet their different NEEDS.
To summarize the Model of Human Behavior (in DISC order):"

### Vibecode Agent
*4/2/2026, 11:25:02 AM*

The C quadrant arc has an incorrect sweep direction. For the lower-left quadrant, going from bottom (90°) to left (180°) should use `sweep-flag=1` (clockwise in SVG), not `0`. The current `0` creates a 270° arc conflict with `large-arc-flag=0`.

---
### System — `Executing tool: Edit`
*4/2/2026, 11:25:05 AM*

Edit: EbookLesson3.tsx

---
### Vibecode Agent
*4/2/2026, 11:25:17 AM*

The C quadrant arc was using `sweep-flag=0` (counter-clockwise), which creates a 270° arc that conflicts with the `large-arc-flag=0` (minor arc) setting. Changing it to `sweep-flag=1` (clockwise) correctly draws the 90° lower-left quadrant from bottom to left, matching how D, I, and S quadrants are drawn. The amber color should now fill properly.

Neither of you wants to back down, give up or compromise.\", strategies: \"Don't force issues. Allow this person to have some choices, control and authority. Don't argue or give ultimatums. Be direct and stick to business.\" },\n    I: { strengths: \"Both of you are fast paced. This person may desire to please you and follow your leadership.\", struggles: \"Your focus on getting things done can clash with this person's desire to have fun and \\\"take-life-as-it-comes.\\\" This person does not share your drive to complete tasks. This person's focus is on people rather than on tasks.\", strategies: \"Realize that this person does not usually focus on one thing, rather he or she focuses on many things. Help Inspiring (I) types finish tasks by working WITH them. Make things FUN! Allow them to talk and socialize. Be positive and willing to express approval to them. Be accepting of their expressions of emotion and feelings.\" },\n    S: { strengths: \"You like to lead, and this personality type likes to follow and help. A supportive person will feel secure as long as you show controlled, stable behavior.\", struggles: \"If you come on too strong, this person can feel intimidated and will take it personally. You may misunderstand this person's softhearted, easygoing nature as being \\\"weak.\\\" Never confuse kindness for weakness. Remember that this person's focus is on people, and he or she tends to be slower paced.\", strategies: \"Be patient and willing to spell things out, step-by-step when working on tasks. Communicate in a calmer, softer manner. Relax, and do not push. Express appreciation often. Be sincere.\" },\n    C: { strengths: \"Both of you focus on tasks and enjoy working independently. With this person's attention to detail, you can accomplish a lot together.\", struggles: \"You tend to move quickly, whereas this person likes to think things through. Your focus is to get things done now, and his or her focus is to get things done right. Your desire to control things may discourage a cautious person, because this person does not like to feel pressured.\", strategies: \"Do not rush or push this person. Do not criticize a cautious person. Be patient, and give him or her time to make decisions. Be willing to answer this person's questions and provide information in a polite manner. Do not expect this person to be a risk-taker like you.\" },\n  },\n  I: {\n    D: { strengths: \"You both are outgoing and activity-driven. Both of you like to win. You may admire the strengths and achievements of this person.\", struggles: \"You may find the other person to be too controlling, while you are too permissive. You are more social, while the other person is more task-driven.\", strategies: \"Understand that a D-type person is direct and results-oriented. Be more direct and get to the point with him or her. Do not be afraid of confrontation. Expect it, and do not take it personally. Work first, and THEN have fun.\" },\n    I: { strengths: \"Both of you live enthusiastically and enjoy being with people. You both like to have fun and tend to forgive easily.\", struggles: \"Both of you tend to live emotionally and may compete for attention. You both are impulsive, and that can lead to challenges with following through with responsibilities and staying organized.\", strategies: \"Remember to listen to the other person, because he or she likes to talk as much as you do. When working on important tasks, keep each other accountable, and be clear about who is responsible for what. Give sincere recognition of this person's abilities, ideas and contributions.\" },\n    S: { strengths: \"Both of you are people-oriented. You like to talk and the other person likes to listen. You tend to get along very well.\", struggles: \"Most struggles are related to pace. You like things fast-paced, exciting, spontaneous and with high energy. The other person likes things to be more calm, quiet and predictable.\", strategies: \"Slow down your approach. Tone down your enthusiasm. Be sincere with praise and appreciation. Do not embarrass this person in public. Allow this person time to warm up and open up to you.\" },\n    C: { strengths: \"Your opposite strengths provide a good balance to each other's weaknesses. You can learn from the other person's analytical nature, and this person can learn not to take things so seriously and to have more fun.\", struggles: \"Your differences can lead to misunderstandings. You love to talk and be on the go, but the other person likes time alone. You are much more verbal, and it is easy for you to miss the other person's more indirect way of sharing concerns.\", strategies: \"Tone down your emotional reactions. Be more factual and objective, especially in the face of conflict. Do not rush or push this person. Be specific in your communication, and expect this person to speak literally and to take things literally.\" },\n  },\n  S: {\n    D: { strengths: \"You are a good supporter and encourager for this driven person who seeks to achieve and exert leadership.\", struggles: \"This person can exhaust you by being controlling or by expecting instant action. You like to relax and go slow, but the other person does everything with a sense of urgency. You can become stressed, and this dominant person can become impatient.\", strategies: \"Do not take it personally when this person takes action without you. Be more firm and results-oriented with this person. Be more direct, decisive and action-oriented when you deal with this person.\" },\n    I: { strengths: \"You tend to get along well, because you are both people-oriented. You both provide praise and appreciation to each other which you each need to feel good about yourselves.\", struggles: \"Your biggest struggle will be keeping up with the pace of this person. The I-type likes excitement and activity, but you like things to be slower and calmer. Inspiring (I) type people have a large social circle, and that can seem overwhelming to you.\", strategies: \"Be more outgoing and energetic with this person. An inspiring person is very impulsive. Be careful not to let this person talk you into doing something you do not want to do. Set some limits and do not feel pressured by this person's energy. Realize that he or she moves quickly, so do not take it personally if this person seems to give you fragmented attention.\" },\n    S: { strengths: \"You have a lot in common and enjoy being with each other. Both of you like a relaxed, personal atmosphere.\", struggles: \"The main struggle is in the area of communication. You both talk indirectly and do not insist on your own way. Neither of you likes to make hard decisions. Neither of you likes conflict or tension, so you avoid bringing up unpleasant issues.\", strategies: \"Be willing to take more initiative and to be more decisive. Realize that some conflict and change is healthy. Draw out how the other person feels, and be willing to honestly share how you feel. Do not hide hurt feelings. Get issues and feelings out on the table and discuss them.\" },\n    C: { strengths: \"Both of you are slower paced. Neither of you is \\\"pushy\\\" and you both prefer to avoid conflict. You can enjoy being together without a lot of conversation.\", struggles: \"You tend to be sensitive while the other person has a tendency toward being critical. Your feelings-oriented nature can clash with the other person's logic-oriented nature. You want warm relationships, but the other person can seem more cold and impersonal to you.\", strategies: \"Do not take this person's questioning, critical nature personally. This person likes to think deeply and analyze everything. Be willing to give in-depth answers. Do not push this person into closeness. Realize that this person is more Task-oriented than People-oriented, so he or she may not be as warm or sensitive as you are.\" },\n  },\n  C: {\n    D: { strengths: \"Both of you share a similar view toward accomplishing tasks. As long as you share the same goals, you can be very effective as a team.\", struggles: \"You may have conflict if you take different approaches to accomplishing a task. You want things done \\\"right,\\\" and this person is focused on getting things done quickly. They may think that you are over-analyzing things, while you may think that he or she is being too hasty.\", strategies: \"Accept the fact that the D-type person needs to have some control and the ability to take some action. Do not criticize or expect perfection. Instead, be willing to recognize and affirm this person's accomplishments.\" },\n    I: { strengths: \"Your strengths balance each other out. You need the other person's freshness and fun, and the other person needs your discipline and logic.\", struggles: \"Because you are opposites in personality, you may have a hard time understanding each other. You may not relate to this person's talkative, outgoing nature, and he or she may not relate to your analytical, cautious nature. Your standards may be too high for this person. You may naturally withhold the praise on which this person thrives.\", strategies: \"You must modify your expectations of this person. Realize that this person will never have the attention to detail that you do. Look for this person's strengths, and be generous with recognition and approval. Listen enthusiastically to his or her stories. Most of all, do not push for perfection or this person may become discouraged and quit.\" },\n    S: { strengths: \"Both of you like to take things slowly. You both enjoy a low-key relationship that is free from conflict.\", struggles: \"You may become frustrated when this person does not appear to think things through the way you do or share your enthusiasm for key details. This person is feelings-oriented, so you may come across as cold and impersonal.\", strategies: \"Be aware of your focus on doing tasks correctly. This person's focus is on peace and security in relationships. Be more warm and personal. Be careful not to criticize. Show sincere appreciation for any effort that is made. Do not set your standards too high, or this person may feel inadequate or simply give up.\" },\n    C: { strengths: \"Both of you like to work hard on projects and focus on details and quality. You both tend to be serious and factual in your conversations.\", struggles: \"There can be trouble when of you disagree on what is \\\"right.\\\" Both of you can quickly shut down and withdraw. You both tend to wage a war of indirect communication.\", strategies: \"Be open and flexible when this person suggests a different way of doing something. Be very careful with any criticism, because you know that criticism of your own work is one of your greatest fears. Do not set your standards so high that the other person feels he or she may not be able to reach them. Be specific with words of encouragement. Tell this person exactly what he or she did correctly and why you liked it.\" },\n  },\n};\n```\n\n## New PersonSection design\n\nEach person section should:\n1. Show the person's name (from gs1_personNName)\n2. Show the person's DISC type badge (from gs3_pNStyle) — read-only, colored\n3. If both `userType` and `personType` are set: display the auto-filled strengths/struggles/strategies as styled read-only cards (NOT editable inputs)\n4. If either type is missing: show a friendly message like \"Complete your DISC style in Growth Sheet # 3 to see your relationship insights.\" with a muted style\n\nThe three sections (strengths, struggles, strategies) should use colored labels:\n- Strengths: `text-disc-s`\n- Struggles: `text-disc-d`  \n- Strategies: `text-disc-i`\n\nShow the user's own type prominently at the top of the sheet (e.g. \"Your DISC style: D\" with the colored badge). If not set, show a prompt.\n\n## Updated instruction text\n\nChange:\n- The intro paragraph: Instead of \"answer the questions below. Again, refer back to the list of names...\" change it to something like: \"Based on the DISC styles you identified in Growth Sheet # 3, we've automatically filled in how you relate to each person. Refer to each specific chart, but feel free to use your own words.\" Replace with: \"Review your personalized relationship insights for each person below. These are based on your DISC style and each person's style from Growth Sheet # 3.\"\n- Section 2 (person 4) instruction: Update to match — \"Review your relationship insights for the person with whom you have a difficult relationship. Understanding these dynamics can help you navigate this relationship more effectively.\"\n\n## Keys to use\n- User's type: `answers.gs3_yourStyle`\n- Person 1 type: `answers.gs3_p1Style`\n- Person 2 type: `answers.gs3_p2Style`\n- Person 3 type: `answers.gs3_p3Style`\n- Person 4 type: `answers.gs3_p4Style`\n- Person names: `answers.gs1_person1Name` through `answers.gs1_person4Name`\n\n## Important\n- Remove ALL editable inputs (no more text inputs for strengths/struggles/strategies)\n- Remove the DiscSelector component entirely\n- Remove the gs4_pNStyle, gs4_pNStrength, gs4_pNStruggle, gs4_pNStrategy keys — no longer needed\n- Keep all the completion section at the bottom (Congratulations, Highlights, closing text) exactly as-is\n- Import: `import { useEbookAnswers } from \"@/hooks/useEbookAnswers\";`\n- Keep the same header/title styling (GS badge, disc-c colors, Growth Sheet # 4 title box)\n","subagent_type":"vibecode-webapp-plugin:frontend-developer"}

You tend to get along very well.\", struggles: \"Most struggles are related to pace. You like things fast-paced, exciting, spontaneous and with high energy. The other person likes things to be more calm, quiet and predictable.\", strategies: \"Slow down your approach. Tone down your enthusiasm. Be sincere with praise and appreciation. Do not embarrass this person in public. Allow this person time to warm up and open up to you.\" },\n    C: { strengths: \"Your opposite strengths provide a good balance to each other's weaknesses. You can learn from the other person's analytical nature, and this person can learn not to take things so seriously and to have more fun.\", struggles: \"Your differences can lead to misunderstandings. You love to talk and be on the go, but the other person likes time alone. You are much more verbal, and it is easy for you to miss the other person's more indirect way of sharing concerns.\", strategies: \"Tone down your emotional reactions. Be more factual and objective, especially in the face of conflict. Do not rush or push this person. Be specific in your communication, and expect this person to speak literally and to take things literally.\" },\n  },\n  S: {\n    D: { strengths: \"You are a good supporter and encourager for this driven person who seeks to achieve and exert leadership.\", struggles: \"This person can exhaust you by being controlling or by expecting instant action. You like to relax and go slow, but the other person does everything with a sense of urgency. You can become stressed, and this dominant person can become impatient.\", strategies: \"Do not take it personally when this person takes action without you. Be more firm and results-oriented with this person. Be more direct, decisive and action-oriented when you deal with this person.\" },\n    I: { strengths: \"You tend to get along well, because you are both people-oriented. You both provide praise and appreciation to each other which you each need to feel good about yourselves.\", struggles: \"Your biggest struggle will be keeping up with the pace of this person. The I-type likes excitement and activity, but you like things to be slower and calmer. Inspiring (I) type people have a large social circle, and that can seem overwhelming to you.\", strategies: \"Be more outgoing and energetic with this person. An inspiring person is very impulsive. Be careful not to let this person talk you into doing something you do not want to do. Set some limits and do not feel pressured by this person's energy. Realize that he or she moves quickly, so do not take it personally if this person seems to give you fragmented attention.\" },\n    S: { strengths: \"You have a lot in common and enjoy being with each other. Both of you like a relaxed, personal atmosphere.\", struggles: \"The main struggle is in the area of communication. You both talk indirectly and do not insist on your own way. Neither of you likes to make hard decisions. Neither of you likes conflict or tension, so you avoid bringing up unpleasant issues.\", strategies: \"Be willing to take more initiative and to be more decisive. Realize that some conflict and change is healthy. Draw out how the other person feels, and be willing to honestly share how you feel. Do not hide hurt feelings. Get issues and feelings out on the table and discuss them.\" },\n    C: { strengths: \"Both of you are slower paced. Neither of you is \\\"pushy\\\" and you both prefer to avoid conflict. You can enjoy being together without a lot of conversation.\", struggles: \"You tend to be sensitive while the other person has a tendency toward being critical. Your feelings-oriented nature can clash with the other person's logic-oriented nature. You want warm relationships, but the other person can seem more cold and impersonal to you.\", strategies: \"Do not take this person's questioning, critical nature personally. This person likes to think deeply and analyze everything. Be willing to give in-depth answers. Do not push this person into closeness. Realize that this person is more Task-oriented than People-oriented, so he or she may not be as warm or sensitive as you are.\" },\n  },\n  C: {\n    D: { strengths: \"Both of you share a similar view toward accomplishing tasks. As long as you share the same goals, you can be very effective as a team.\", struggles: \"You may have conflict if you take different approaches to accomplishing a task. You want things done \\\"right,\\\" and this person is focused on getting things done quickly. They may think that you are over-analyzing things, while you may think that he or she is being too hasty.\", strategies: \"Accept the fact that the D-type person needs to have some control and the ability to take some action. Do not criticize or expect perfection. Instead, be willing to recognize and affirm this person's accomplishments.\" },\n    I: { strengths: \"Your strengths balance each other out. You need the other person's freshness and fun, and the other person needs your discipline and logic.\", struggles: \"Because you are opposites in personality, you may have a hard time understanding each other. You may not relate to this person's talkative, outgoing nature, and he or she may not relate to your analytical, cautious nature. Your standards may be too high for this person. You may naturally withhold the praise on which this person thrives.\", strategies: \"You must modify your expectations of this person. Realize that this person will never have the attention to detail that you do. Look for this person's strengths, and be generous with recognition and approval. Listen enthusiastically to his or her stories. Most of all, do not push for perfection or this person may become discouraged and quit.\" },\n    S: { strengths: \"Both of you like to take things slowly. You both enjoy a low-key relationship that is free from conflict.\", struggles: \"You may become frustrated when this person does not appear to think things through the way you do or share your enthusiasm for key details. This person is feelings-oriented, so you may come across as cold and impersonal.\", strategies: \"Be aware of your focus on doing tasks correctly. This person's focus is on peace and security in relationships. Be more warm and personal. Be careful not to criticize. Show sincere appreciation for any effort that is made. Do not set your standards too high, or this person may feel inadequate or simply give up.\" },\n    C: { strengths: \"Both of you like to work hard on projects and focus on details and quality. You both tend to be serious and factual in your conversations.\", struggles: \"There can be trouble when of you disagree on what is \\\"right.\\\" Both of you can quickly shut down and withdraw. You both tend to wage a war of indirect communication.\", strategies: \"Be open and flexible when this person suggests a different way of doing something. Be very careful with any criticism, because you know that criticism of your own work is one of your greatest fears. Do not set your standards so high that the other person feels he or she may not be able to reach them. Be specific with words of encouragement. Tell this person exactly what he or she did correctly and why you liked it.\" },\n  },\n};\n```\n\n## New PersonSection design\n\nEach person section should:\n1. Show the person's name (from gs1_personNName) — if missing, show italic muted \"Name not entered yet\"\n2. Show a colored DISC type badge for the person's type (from gs3_pNStyle). The badge should be colored using the disc color tokens: disc-d (green), disc-i (red), disc-s (blue), disc-c (amber). Show the letter and the type name beneath it.\n3. If both `userType` (gs3_yourStyle) and `personType` (gs3_pNStyle) are valid DISC letters: display the auto-filled strengths/struggles/strategies as styled read-only text blocks\n4. If either type is missing: show a friendly muted message like \"Complete your DISC styles in Growth Sheet # 3 to see your personalized relationship insights here.\"\n\nThe three sections (strengths, struggles, strategies) should each have a colored label and the text:\n- \"Strengths\" label: `text-disc-s`\n- \"Struggles\" label: `text-disc-d`\n- \"Strategies\" label: `text-disc-i`\n\n## User's type display\nAt the top of the growth sheet content (before section 1), show a small banner/badge showing the user's DISC type. Example: \"Your DISC style: [D badge]\" — if not set, show \"Set your DISC style in Growth Sheet # 3\". Use the appropriate disc color for the badge.\n\n## Updated instruction text\n\nReplace the existing intro paragraph with:\n\"Based on the DISC styles you identified in Growth Sheet # 3, we've automatically filled in how you relate to each person. Read through each section to discover your natural strengths, likely struggles, and practical strategies for each relationship.\"\n\nSection 1 heading text (after the \"1.\" heading): Replace with:\n\"Review your personalized relationship insights for each person below. These are based on your DISC style and each person's style from Growth Sheet # 3.\"\n\nSection 2 (person 4) instruction: Replace with:\n\"Review your relationship insights for the person with whom you have a difficult relationship. Understanding these dynamics can help you navigate this relationship more effectively.\"\n\n## Keys to use\n- User's type: `answers.gs3_yourStyle`\n- Person 1 type: `answers.gs3_p1Style`\n- Person 2 type: `answers.gs3_p2Style`\n- Person 3 type: `answers.gs3_p3Style`\n- Person 4 type: `answers.gs3_p4Style`\n- Person names: `answers.gs1_person1Name` through `answers.gs1_person4Name`\n\n## Important rules\n- Remove ALL editable text inputs\n- Remove the DiscSelector component entirely\n- Remove the StyleKey type and PersonFields interface — no longer needed\n- Keep all the completion section at the bottom EXACTLY as-is (Congratulations box, Highlights to Remember list, closing text paragraphs, \"- Robert A. Rohm, Ph.D.\" sign-off)\n- Import only: `import { useEbookAnswers } from \"@/hooks/useEbookAnswers\";`\n- Keep the same header/title styling: GS badge, disc-c colors, \"Growth Sheet # 4\" title box, divider line\n- The DISC_COLORS map: `{ D: \"disc-d\", I: \"disc-i\", S: \"disc-s\", C: \"disc-c\" }`\n- DISC type names: `{ D: \"Dominant\", I: \"Inspiring\", S: \"Supportive\", C: \"Cautious\" }`\n","subagent_type":"vibecode-webapp-plugin:frontend-developer"}
